analysis:
- Common skeleton: nearly every high-scoring heuristic follows the same four-stage pipeline — nondominated-prefilter → cheap per-point scoring (box volume ± scalarization) → small deterministic shortlist → greedy/CELF selection using exact hv on the shortlist, then bounded local 1-for-1 swaps. That pattern concentrates expensive pygmo.hypervolume calls where they matter.
- Stronger variants (1–5) minimize hv calls via: small shortlists, cached singleton HVs, CELF lazy timestamps, and incremental local swaps. Heuristic 1 is notable for computing exact gains only on a tiny shortlist and then doing cached incremental swaps — low-call footprint and deterministic.
- Mid/weak variants often re-run full HV computations in inner loops (Heuristics 6,7,11), compute many redundant O(N^2) dominance/HV operations, or use heavy precomputation (Heuristic 9 pairwise HV matrix, Heuristic 10 huge weight sets) that blow up cost for larger N. Some also shuffle or use RNG unnecessarily, adding nondeterminism without clear benefit.
- Avoidable overheads seen: repeated full-set hv.compute calls (instead of incremental deltas), recomputing dominance/rect volumes inside loops, large shortlists not justified by marginal gain, excessive pairwise HV precomputations, and not using cheap upper bounds to prune candidate evaluations.
- Data reuse opportunities underused: reuse singleton and pairwise HVs more selectively; keep per-candidate upper bounds (box-volume or scalarized maxima) to skip exact evaluation; maintain incremental union/hv structure so swap trials only recompute localized changes. Vectorized batch scoring (rect_vol, scalarization) is done well in several heuristics — keep that.
- Tradeoffs: more weight vectors or pairwise precomputation can improve selection quality but cost scales poorly; CELF + cached singletons gives most quality per HV call. Local search budgets should be small, targeted, and deterministic to keep runtime predictable.
experience: Prefer CELF with cached singletons, adaptive shortlist size (function of Pareto size and k), and conservative pairwise caching only when shortlist ≲ 40. Use cheap upper bounds to prune exact hv calls, incremental hv updates for swaps, and parallelize independent hv evaluations. Tune swap budget and shortlist adaptively to maximize hypervolume per hv-call.
current_reflection: Prefer CELF with cached singletons, adaptive shortlist size (function of Pareto size and k), and conservative pairwise caching only when shortlist ≲ 40. Use cheap upper bounds to prune exact hv calls, incremental hv updates for swaps, and parallelize independent hv evaluations. Tune swap budget and shortlist adaptively to maximize hypervolume per hv-call.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
bad_reflections: Prefer deterministic shortlisting + a lazy-greedy core: (1) fast Pareto filter, (2) cheap vectorized scoring to pick a small candidate pool ≈ O(k)–O(k·log k), (3) CELF-style greedy using cached singleton HVs and current_hv to avoid repeated full HV recomputes, (4) bounded local swaps that only recompute HV for affected replacements. Tune shortlist size proportional to k and cache everything.

Focus on deterministic Pareto prefiltering, build a shortlist scaled to k via fast box/weight proxies, then run CELF lazy greedy using cached singleton HVs and timestamps; compute exact HV only on shortlist, and finish with a bounded 1-for-1 swap loop. Use sets for lookups, vectorized diffs, and precompute acos/pairwise HVs only if shortlist is tiny. This keeps quality high and runtime predictable.

Prefer small deterministic shortlists from cheap vectorized proxies, cache singleton (and selective pairwise) HVs, use CELF lazy updates to limit exact HV calls, and only verify promising candidates with exact HV. Bound swap iterations and shortlist size. Favor reproducible tie‑breakers and incremental HV updates; avoid full N^2 HV recomputations or dense pairwise HVs unless N is tiny.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: CELF, cached singleton HVCs, adaptive shortlist (f(Pareto,k)), pairwise cache ≤40, cheap upper bounds, incremental HV deltas, parallel eval, deterministic seed, bounded swaps
- Advice: Prefilter Pareto front, build a small deterministic shortlist via cheap vectorized proxies or few-weight HVCs, run CELF lazy greedy using cached singleton HVCs and upper bounds, perform bounded local 1-for-1 swaps with incremental HV updates, parallelize independent exact HV calls, and adapt shortlist size and swap budget to maximize hypervolume per HV-call.
- Avoid: Dense pairwise caches or N^2 HV recomputations, per-candidate Python-side HV loops, non‑deterministic tie breakers, and unbounded swap loops.
- Explanation: Combine aggressive shortlisting and cheap bounds to cut exact HV calls; cache singletons and use incremental deltas for fast greedy/swaps; only pay pairwise/parallel costs when shortlist is tiny.
